title: MongoDB Query Operator Injection from User Input
description: MongoDB query operators ($gt, $lt, $ne, $in, etc.) are constructed from unsanitized user input, allowing attackers to manipulate query logic. For example, sending {"$ne": null} as a password field bypasses authentication by matching any non-null password.
remediation: Use express-mongo-sanitize middleware to strip $ operators from user input. Explicitly extract and validate expected fields from req.body instead of passing the entire object. Cast inputs to expected types with String(), Number(), etc.

title: MongoDB $where Clause with User-Controlled JavaScript
description: The $where operator in MongoDB evaluates JavaScript server-side. When user input is concatenated into a $where clause, attackers can inject arbitrary JavaScript that executes on the database server, leading to data exfiltration, denial of service, or remote code execution.
remediation: Avoid $where entirely. Replace $where queries with standard query operators ($eq, $gt, $regex, etc.) or aggregation pipelines. If $where is unavoidable, never include user input in the expression string.

title: MongoDB $regex Operator from Unsanitized User Input
description: User input is passed directly into a MongoDB $regex operator or JavaScript RegExp constructor without escaping special characters. Attackers can craft malicious regular expressions to cause ReDoS (Regular Expression Denial of Service) or bypass input filters.
remediation: Escape all special regex characters from user input before using it in $regex. Use a library like escape-string-regexp. Consider using $text search with text indexes instead of $regex for user-facing search features.

title: MongoDB Aggregation Pipeline Injection from User Input
description: User-controlled input is used to construct MongoDB aggregation pipeline stages. Attackers can inject stages like $lookup to access other collections, $merge or $out to write data to arbitrary collections, or $graphLookup to traverse relationships and exfiltrate data.
remediation: Never allow users to define aggregation pipeline stages directly. Build pipelines server-side using validated parameters. Whitelist allowed fields and operations. Validate that user input only provides values, not stage operators or collection names.
